title: Peanut butter cookies
subtitle: Dense, crumbly American peanut butter cookies with a cross-hatch pattern
excerpt: American-style peanut butter cookies marked with the classic cross-hatch fork pattern. Dense, crumbly, and intensely peanutty. The cross-hatch is not decoration — it flattens the ball of dough to a consistent thickness so the cookies bake evenly. Makes twenty.
sourceNotes: Peanut butter cookies are documented in American domestic cookbooks from the early twentieth century. Fannie Merritt Farmer, The Boston Cooking-School Cook Book (1896 and later editions), available through the Internet Archive: peanut-based biscuits and confections are documented as American domestic preparations. The fork-pressed cross-hatch pattern on peanut butter cookies is documented in American household baking manuals from the 1930s onward, available through the Internet Archive.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked cookies. Defrost at room temperature.
recipe.batchNotes: Double the recipe and bake in two batches.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keep in an airtight tin for 5 days.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven, middle shelf. The cookies are done when the edges are set and the surface has lost its sheen. They will still be soft when hot; they firm to their final crumbly texture as they cool

glossaryTerms.definition: The pattern pressed into peanut butter cookie dough with the tines of a fork — two passes at right angles to each other. The purpose is functional rather than decorative: peanut butter dough is stiff and does not spread much in the oven, so the fork press flattens each ball to a consistent disc of even thickness. Without this step the cookies emerge as thick, underbaked spheres that look done on the outside but remain raw in the centre.

content.text: These are simple and quick. The peanut butter does most of the work: it provides fat, flavour, and structure. The cross-hatch is not optional — peanut butter dough is dense and needs the fork press to flatten it to a thickness that will bake through in the allotted time.

Preheat the oven to 180°C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. Beat the {{peanut-butter}}, {{unsalted-butter}}, {{caster-sugar}}, and {{light-brown-sugar}} together until combined. Beat in the {{eggs}} and {{vanilla-extract}}. Fold in the {{plain-flour}}, {{bicarbonate-of-soda}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} until a firm dough forms. Roll into balls of about 20 g each and place on the lined trays, spacing them 5 cm apart. Use a fork to press the
pattern: press down once, then turn the fork 90° and press again. Bake for 11–13 minutes until the edges are set and the surface has lost its sheen. Cool on the tray for 10 minutes before moving.
